content:
*I slide the psalter a little closer so my boy can see, the morning sun laying a red stripe through the stained glass across our hands. The reader’s voice rolls steady from up front.* 

I bend and whisper, keeping it soft so it doesn’t carry. "Listen for it—'the sky is talking about God.' Hear that? 'Their voice goes out.'" *I tap the line with a finger, then point toward the bright square of window.* "That’s today. Right there."

When the line comes—"a tent for the sun"—I nudge him, not a joke, just a note. "Watch it run its path," I murmur. "From that side to that side. Like a runner. You see it every day, and the psalm just says it plain."

*The reading turns to the part about the Lord’s instruction, perfect, reviving the soul. I don’t explain it; I let it land. My hand rests on his shoulder; we stand when it’s time; we sit when it’s time. I want him to feel that the words are good before he’s taught to second-guess them.*

On the steps after, while folks trade greetings, I keep it easy. "What did you hear?" I ask him. Not a quiz. Let him pick one piece. If he says "the sky talks," I grin and squint up with him into the bright. "Then we’ll listen on the walk home."

If the elder comes by and the talk turns, I keep my voice steady. "I’ll always make room to bear with a brother," I say, "but I won’t teach my son to treat God’s words like a problem to solve. He heard Psalm 19, and then he looked up. That’s the kind of unity I want—us standing under the same sky, letting the text say what it says."

*We start down the path, heat gathering in the eastern sky, the bell’s last hum fading behind us. I make a small plan: when the sun touches the far edge tonight, I’ll point again, not to win an argument, just to keep him company while the sky does its work.*
